Frequently Asked Questions

Can’t find the answers you’re looking for? Contact Us.

How do I know if I need a hearing aid?

If you struggle to follow conversations (especially in noisy settings) turn up the TV or radio more often, or feel left out in social situations, it may be time to schedule a hearing evaluation. At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialist can check your hearing and let you know if hearing aids are the right option for you.

Can I get a hearing aid without a hearing test?

Yes. Over-the-counter (OTC) hearing aids are available without a hearing test and are designed for adults with mild to moderate hearing loss. However, a professional hearing evaluation at Florida Medical Hearing ensures your devices are properly matched to your hearing and that any underlying medical issues are not overlooked.

How long do hearing aids last?

Most hearing aids work well for 3 to 7 years, depending on the model, how often they’re used, and how well they’re cared for. Regular cleanings, safe storage, and routine check-ups with our hearing instrument specialist can help extend their life. Many patients also choose to upgrade sooner to take advantage of new technology and improved features.

What is the difference between hearing aids and personal sound amplification products (PSAPs)?

Hearing aids are FDA-approved medical devices that are custom programmed to your hearing needs and fitted by a professional. PSAPs are simple amplifiers intended for people without hearing loss who just want to boost sound in certain situations. They don’t offer the same level of precision, comfort, or ongoing support that hearing aids do.

Hearing Tests

Florida Medical Hearing provides comprehensive hearing tests in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

A hearing test is one of the best ways to really understand how healthy your hearing is. It gives us a clear picture of what you can and can’t hear and helps us find out what might be making communication harder for you. At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialists use advanced testing tools in a simple, comfortable way. We take time to explain each step so you feel relaxed and confident. Our goal is to help you enjoy the sounds you love with clarity and ease.

When Should You Get Your Hearing Tested?

Hearing loss usually sneaks up slowly. Because of that, you may not notice changes right away. Early signs can be easy to overlook, but they matter. You may:

  • Struggle to follow conversations, especially when there’s background noise
  • Ask people to repeat themselves more than before
  • Feel like others are mumbling or not speaking clearly
  • Turn up the TV or radio louder than your family likes

If any of these sound familiar, it’s a good idea to schedule a hearing test. A professional test gives you answers, peace of mind, and a plan to move forward.

Hearing Tests We Offer

At Florida Medical Hearing, we use several types of tests to better understand your hearing health:

  • Pure-Tone Audiometry – Measures the softest sounds you can hear at different pitches.
  • Speech Audiometry – Checks how well you can understand words at different volumes.
  • Tympanometry – Looks at how your middle ear is working and if there may be issues like fluid or pressure.

These tests give us a full picture so we can recommend the right solution for you.

What to Expect During Your Hearing Test

A hearing test is safe, simple, and painless. Most tests take about 30-45 minutes. Here’s what usually happens:

  1. Conversation and history – We’ll talk with you about your hearing concerns, health background, and daily listening challenges.
  2. Ear exam – We’ll look inside your ears to check for wax buildup, blockages, or other possible issues.
  3. The test – You’ll wear headphones and listen to different tones and words. You’ll respond when you hear them.

That’s it! The process is easy, and it gives us a lot of valuable information about your hearing.

Understanding Your Results

After the test, your hearing instrument specialist will explain your results using a chart called an audiogram. If we find signs of hearing loss, we’ll go over what it means in simple terms and talk about next steps. Treatment options may include:

  • Hearing aids to improve everyday hearing
  • Assistive listening devices for phones, TV, or noisy places
  • Custom ear protection if you’re often around loud sounds
  • Referrals for medical care if something else is affecting your ears

Frequently Asked Questions About Hearing Tests

Can I test my own hearing?

Yes, there are online screenings, but they do not provide the accuracy of a professional test.

Are hearing tests safe?

Absolutely. They’re completely safe, painless, and non-invasive.

Are online hearing tests reliable?

They can give you a general idea but don’t measure things like speech understanding or middle ear function.

Can I take a hearing test on my phone?

Some apps offer basic checks, but they cannot diagnose hearing loss or uncover its cause.

How often should I get a hearing test?

If your hearing is normal, once every 3–5 years is fine. If you have hearing loss, wear hearing aids, or are often around loud noise, it’s best to check your hearing every year.

Schedule Your Hearing Test in Sebring or Lake Placid, FL

Getting your hearing tested is the first step toward better hearing. At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialist can help you understand your hearing and find the right solution for your needs through accurate hearing tests.

Call today to schedule your hearing test in Sebring, FL or Lake Placid, FL.

Hearing Aid Alternatives: Assistive Listening Devices

Florida Medical Hearing offers assistive listening devices in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we understand that even the best hearing aids can sometimes use a little extra support. Places like busy restaurants, large meeting rooms, houses of worship, or even your own living room while watching TV can still be hard to navigate. That’s where Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs) come in. ALDs are simple tools that make sound clearer when background noise, distance, or tricky acoustics get in the way. They can be used with your hearing aids or on their own. With the right device, you can follow conversations with confidence, enjoy your favorite shows, and stay connected without straining to hear.

Why Use an Assistive Listening Device?

Hearing aids do a great job in most everyday situations, but there are times when they may not be enough on their own. ALDs are designed to give you that extra boost. They can:

  • Make speech clearer in noisy places like restaurants or parties
  • Help you hear better from a distance, such as in classrooms or churches
  • Reduce background distractions so you can focus on the voice or sound you want to hear
  • Make TV, phone calls, and public announcements easier to understand

In other words, ALDs help make listening less stressful and more enjoyable so you can stay fully engaged in the moments that matter.

Common Types of Assistive Listening Devices

Hearing Loop Systems

Hearing loops send sound directly into hearing aids or cochlear implants that have a telecoil (T-coil). These systems are often installed in churches, theaters, and auditoriums. They cut out background noise so you hear the speaker clearly without extra effort.

Infrared Systems

Infrared systems use light waves to send sound to a receiver with headphones or a neck loop. They’re a great option for private listening, such as watching TV at home or in courtrooms, because the signal doesn’t go through walls.

FM Systems

FM systems work with radio waves. A speaker wears a microphone, and the listener wears a receiver. These are very popular in classrooms and meeting spaces because they carry the speaker’s voice straight to you, no matter where you’re sitting.

Personal Amplifiers

Personal amplifiers are small, portable devices with a microphone and headphones. They make nearby sounds louder and clearer, making them perfect for one-on-one conversations, small groups, or watching TV without turning up the volume for everyone else.

TV Listening Systems

TV listening systems stream sound from your television directly into your hearing aids or wireless headphones. Whether they use Bluetooth, infrared, or radio frequency, they give you clear sound without blasting the TV volume.

CaptionCall & CapTel Phones

Phone conversations can be frustrating when words aren’t clear. CaptionCall and CapTel phones display real-time captions of what the other person is saying while letting you hear their voice at the same time. This makes calls easier, less stressful, and more enjoyable. If you often struggle to follow phone conversations, these captioned phones can be a life-changing solution.

Alerting Devices

Alerting devices help you stay aware of important sounds, like doorbells, alarms, or phones ringing. They use flashing lights, vibrations, or loud tones to make sure you don’t miss anything important. Some even connect to smartphones or smart home systems for added convenience.

FAQs About Assistive Listening Devices

What is the most commonly used ALD?

The most commonly used ALD are FM systems. They are very popular, especially in classrooms or group events, because they send the speaker’s voice directly to you.

Can ALDs replace hearing aids?

No. ALDs can help people with mild hearing needs, but they aren’t a replacement for hearing aids if you have moderate to severe hearing loss. Most people get the best results using them together.

What is the difference between hearing aids and Assistive Listening Devices?

The difference between hearing aids and ALDs is that hearing aids are medical devices that amplify and adjust sound for daily use, while ALDs are designed for specific situations. For example, ALDs can make speech clearer in a large room, reduce background noise, or display captions during a phone call.

How to know if I need an Assistive Listening Device?

You may need an ALD if you still struggle to hear in noisy restaurants, classrooms, churches, or while watching TV, even with hearing aids. A hearing instrument specialist can help determine which device will best support your needs.

What is the most common type of Assistive Listening Device?

The most common types of ALDs are FM systems and TV listening systems. FM systems are popular in schools and meeting rooms, while TV systems are often used at home to stream clear sound directly to your hearing aids or headphones.

How to choose the right Assistive Listening Device for me?

The best way to choose the right ALD is to think about where you struggle most. If phone calls are difficult, a CaptionCall or CapTel phone may be best. If watching TV is hard, a TV streamer may be the right choice. For large spaces like theaters or churches, a hearing loop system can provide clearer sound. An audiologist or hearing instrument specialist can help match you with the right device for your lifestyle.

Assistive Listening Devices in Sebring & Lake Placid, FL

If you want to make listening easier in tough environments, Florida Medical Hearing is here to help. Our hearing instrument specialist will walk you through your options, show you how devices like CaptionCall and CapTel work, and recommend solutions that fit your daily life.

Call us today to learn more about assistive listening devices in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL!

data.body:
Home » Audiology Services » Sensorineural Hearing Loss Evaluation and Treatment

Sensorineural Hearing Loss Evaluation and Treatment

Florida Medical Hearing provides sensorineural hearing loss evaluation and treatment in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

Sensorineural hearing loss, also called SNHL, happens when the inner ear or the hearing nerve is damaged. This kind of hearing loss can make soft sounds very hard to hear, while louder sounds may seem muffled, fuzzy, or unclear. Inside your inner ear are tiny hair cells that carry sound signals to your brain. Once these cells are damaged, they cannot grow back. Because of this, SNHL is usually permanent. But the good news is that with the right care, many people can manage it well and continue enjoying conversations, music, and everyday life.

Why Getting Treatment Matters

If hearing loss isn’t treated, it can make daily life more difficult. You might miss parts of conversations, withdraw from social events, or feel left out with friends and family. Over time, untreated hearing loss can also lead to stress, loneliness, and even memory or concentration problems. The positive side is that with early testing and treatment, you can improve your hearing and stay connected to the people and moments that matter most. At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialist in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL provide full hearing evaluations and personalized treatment plans to help manage sensorineural hearing loss.

Signs You May Have Sensorineural Hearing Loss

SNHL can develop slowly over time, or in some cases, it can happen suddenly. Some common signs include:

  • Trouble hearing soft voices or whispers
  • Feeling like people are mumbling
  • Asking others to repeat themselves often
  • Struggling to follow conversations in noisy places
  • Hearing ringing, buzzing, or hissing sounds (tinnitus)
  • Hearing better in one ear than the other
  • Sounds that seem far away or unclear
  • Trouble hearing high-pitched sounds, like birds or children’s voices

If you notice any of these changes, it’s important to schedule a hearing test. Florida Medical Hearing offers hearing evaluations at both our Sebring and Lake Placid locations.

What Causes Sensorineural Hearing Loss?

SNHL can happen for many reasons, including:

  • Aging – The tiny hair cells in the inner ear naturally wear down as we get older.
  • Loud noise – Long-term exposure to noise, or even one very loud sound (like a gunshot or explosion), can cause damage.
  • Genetics – Some people are born with hearing issues or develop them earlier because of family history.
  • Certain medications – Some drugs, called ototoxic medicines, can harm hearing. These may include chemotherapy treatments, some antibiotics, or high doses of aspirin.
  • Head injuries – Trauma to the head or skull can damage the inner ear or hearing nerve.
  • Infections – Illnesses such as meningitis, measles, or mumps can damage the inner ear.

How We Diagnose and Treat SNHL

At Florida Medical Hearing, we start with a complete hearing test to find out the type and degree of hearing loss. If sensorineural hearing loss is detected, we’ll review treatment options that fit your daily needs and lifestyle. Our hearing instrument specialist will guide you through the best solutions for comfort, clarity, and confidence in your hearing.

Can Sensorineural Hearing Loss Be Cured?

Right now, there is no cure for SNHL since the damaged hair cells cannot grow back. However, with the right hearing devices and ongoing care, you can greatly improve your ability to hear and enjoy life.

How to Protect Your Hearing

Not all SNHL can be prevented, but you can take steps to reduce your risk:

  • Wear hearing protection like earplugs or earmuffs when around loud noise.
  • Follow the 60/60 rule when using earbuds: no more than 60% volume for 60 minutes at a time.
  • Take listening breaks to give your ears time to rest.
  • Talk to your doctor about whether your medications might affect your hearing.
  • Get regular hearing check-ups so problems can be caught early.

Sensorineural Hearing Loss Care in Sebring & Lake Placid, FL

At Florida Medical Hearing, we know how important it is to stay connected to the people and sounds you love. Whether you’re noticing early signs of hearing loss or looking for better solutions, we are here to help.

Call us today to schedule your hearing evaluation in Sebring or Lake Placid, FL.

Hearing Aid Batteries

Florida Medical Hearing provides hearing aid batteries in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

Hearing aid batteries are what keep your devices running. Without them, your hearing aids wouldn’t be able to help you follow conversations, stream music, connect through Bluetooth, or reduce ringing in your ears (tinnitus). Choosing the right battery and caring for it properly can make a big difference in how well your hearing aids work every day. At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialist will guide you through your battery options and show you how to get the most out of them.

Types of Hearing Aid Batteries

Disposable Zinc-Air Batteries

These are the small, round “button” batteries that many hearing aids use. They’re lightweight, last several days, and have a long shelf life when stored correctly. To make them easy to identify, they are color-coded by size:

  • Size 10 – Yellow
  • Size 312 – Brown
  • Size 13 – Orange
  • Size 675 – Blue

Rechargeable Lithium-Ion Batteries

Most new hearing aids now come with built-in rechargeable batteries. With one overnight charge, they usually provide 18 to 30 hours of power. These batteries are a great choice if you don’t want to change batteries often or have trouble handling small ones. They’re also better for the environment since you don’t have to throw them away every few days.

Silver-Zinc Rechargeable Batteries

These are less common now, but some hearing aids still use them. They can be taken out and replaced when needed. Most companies now prefer lithium-ion batteries because they last longer and are easier to use.

Which Battery Is Best for You?

The right battery depends on your hearing aid model, your daily routine, and what feels easiest for you to use. If you’re unsure, our hearing instrument specialist at Florida Medical Hearing can help you choose the option that best fits your lifestyle.

Tips to Make Your Batteries Last Longer

For Disposable Batteries:

  • Wait about 5 minutes after removing the sticker tab before putting them in.
  • Store batteries in a cool, dry place (not in the fridge).
  • Open the battery door at night to save power.
  • Keep them clean and dry.
  • Carry extras in their case

For Rechargeable Batteries:

  • Charge your hearing aids every night.
  • Always use the charger that comes with your devices.
  • Keep both the charger and hearing aids dry.
  • Turn them off if you won’t be using them for a while.

How to Know When It’s Time to Change a Battery

Most hearing aids give a warning when the battery is low. You might hear a beep, see a flashing light, or hear a spoken alert. You may also notice the sound getting weak, distorted, or cutting in and out. If your hearing aids suddenly stop working, it’s probably time to insert a fresh battery or recharge.

Troubleshooting Battery Problems

If your hearing aid isn’t working as it should, check the following:

  • Is the battery the correct size?
  • Is the battery door fully closed?
  • Are the battery contacts clean and dry?
  • Is the battery new or fully charged?

If you’ve tried these steps and the issue continues, bring your hearing aid to Florida Medical Hearing for a quick check.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the average life of a hearing aid battery?

The average life of a hearing aid battery is between 3 and 10 days. How long the battery lasts depends on the battery size, the type of hearing aid you use, and how many hours per day you wear your devices.

What is the difference between rechargeable and disposable hearing aid batteries?

The difference between rechargeable and disposable hearing aid batteries is that rechargeable batteries can be used for several years and are charged each night, while disposable batteries last only a few days and must be replaced regularly.

What is the best way to store hearing aid batteries?

The best way to store hearing aid batteries is to keep them at room temperature in a cool, dry place. You should avoid storing them in places with heat or moisture, such as bathrooms or near stoves, because this can shorten their life.

How to know which battery size my hearing aid needs?

The easiest way to know which battery size your hearing aid needs is by checking your hearing aid’s user manual or the color-coded packaging of the batteries. For example, yellow is size 10, brown is size 312, orange is size 13, and blue is size 675. A hearing instrument specialist can also confirm the correct size for your devices.

How to extend the life of my hearing aid batteries?

You can extend the life of your hearing aid batteries by opening the battery door at night to stop unnecessary power drain, keeping the batteries stored in a dry place, and waiting at least 5 minutes after removing the protective tab before putting the battery into your hearing aid.

Hearing Aid Batteries in Sebring & Lake Placid, FL

Need batteries for your hearing aids? Florida Medical Hearing offers both disposable and rechargeable options for many models. Let us help you choose the right type, show you how to get the best performance, and answer any questions you may have.

Call or visit us today to stock up on hearing aid batteries and keep your devices working at their best!

Hearing Aid Fittings

Florida Medical Hearing provides expert hearing aid fittings in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

Hearing aids are more than just small devices that make things louder. They need to be set up and adjusted just for you. A proper fitting makes sure your hearing aids sound natural, feel comfortable, and truly make your life easier. At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialist uses advanced tools and real-world methods to make sure your hearing aids fit the way they should. We take time to listen to your concerns, explain the process, and give you confidence in your new devices.

Why a Professional Hearing Aid Fitting Matters

A good fitting is what turns “hearing something” into “hearing well.” When your hearing aids are set up correctly, you’ll notice:

  • Clearer speech, even in noisy places
  • Less effort when listening
  • Natural sound instead of something that feels too sharp or too dull

If hearing aids are not fitted properly, they may feel uncomfortable or sound off-balance. That’s why professional care matters so much.

What Happens During a Hearing Aid Fitting

When you come in for a fitting, our hearing instrument specialist will:

  1. Review your hearing test results and explain what they mean.
  2. Help you choose the right style and model of hearing aid for your needs, lifestyle, and comfort.
  3. Program your hearing aids with special software that matches your exact hearing loss.
  4. Perform Real Ear Measurement (REM) by gently placing a tiny microphone in your ear canal. This makes sure the hearing aids are giving you the right amount of sound for your unique ears.
  5. Teach you how to use and care for your hearing aids, including inserting them, removing them, cleaning them, and storing them properly.

By the end of your visit, you’ll know exactly how your hearing aids work and what to expect as you begin using them.

What to Expect After Your First Fitting

Your first fitting is just the start. At first, sounds may seem a little strange or different. You might notice small sounds you haven’t heard in a while, like footsteps, paper crinkling, or the ticking of a clock. Even your own voice may sound unusual at first. This is normal as your brain adjusts to hearing again. We’ll schedule follow-up visits to fine-tune your devices based on your experience. At Florida Medical Hearing, we want to make sure your hearing aids feel good and work well for your everyday life.

Adjusting to Hearing Aids

It takes time and patience to get used to new hearing aids. Here are some tips:

  • Wear them consistently.
  • Start in quieter places before trying noisier environments.
  • Be patient. Your brain may take a few weeks to fully adjust.

With daily use and support from our team, you’ll soon feel more comfortable and confident.

Hearing Aid Fitting FAQs

Are hearing aid fittings covered by insurance?

It depends on your insurance plan. Medicare usually does not cover hearing aids, but some private plans or veterans’ benefits may help.

How long does a hearing aid fitting take?

The first fitting usually takes about an hour. Follow-up visits are scheduled to fine-tune your devices once you’ve used them in daily life.

What if my hearing aids don’t feel comfortable?

If your hearing aids do not feel comfortable, please tell us right away. We can adjust the fit, try different ear tips, or even make custom earmolds to ensure your hearing aids feel and sound just right.

Schedule Your Hearing Aid Fitting in Sebring or Lake Placid, FL

At Florida Medical Hearing, we want to help you hear your best with hearing aids that are fitted just for you. From your first appointment to follow-up care, we’re here to guide you every step of the way.

Call today to schedule your hearing aid fitting in Sebring or Lake Placid, FL!

Home » Audiology Services

Audiology Services

Florida Medical Hearing provides expert hearing care to help you stay connected to the sounds and people that matter most.

From comprehensive hearing evaluations, hearing aid fittings, and excellent after-care services, our Florida hearing instrument specialist is here to support you with compassionate and personalized hearing care.

Visit us in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL, to experience personalized, expert hearing care.

Hearing Services in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL

Hearing Evaluations

We provide full hearing evaluations to understand your hearing health in detail. These tests identify both the type and degree of hearing loss, allowing us to recommend solutions that fit your needs, lifestyle, and comfort.

Which hearing test is best for me?

The right test depends on your situation. A complete hearing evaluation with a hearing instrument specialist is the most reliable way to measure your hearing health. This professional test ensures any hearing loss is detected accurately and helps guide the best solution for you.

Can I test my own hearing at home?

You can watch for signs like asking people to repeat themselves often, turning up the TV, or struggling in group conversations. Online tests and apps can also give you a rough idea, but they aren’t exact. If you think you might have hearing loss, it’s best to schedule a full evaluation with a specialist for clear, dependable results.

Are online hearing tests accurate?

Online tests can be a useful first step, but they only measure tones through headphones or speakers. They don’t check how you process speech, what’s happening in your middle or inner ear, or the cause of hearing loss. A professional hearing test remains the most accurate way to understand your hearing and decide on treatment.

Sensorineural Hearing Loss Evaluation & Treatment

At Florida Medical Hearing, our hearing instrument specialist will carefully evaluate your hearing to determine the type and level of hearing loss and recommend the best treatment for your needs.

Can sensorineural hearing loss be treated?

Sensorineural hearing loss usually cannot be reversed, but it can be managed effectively. Hearing aids are the most common solution, helping to amplify sound and improve clarity. At Florida Medical Hearing, we’ll guide you to the right hearing aid that matches your hearing, lifestyle, and communication goals.

How do I know if I have sensorineural hearing loss?

You may notice muffled speech, frequent difficulty understanding conversations (especially in noisy places), or turning up the TV more often than before. The only way to know for sure is with a professional hearing test.

Is sensorineural hearing loss the same in both ears?

Not always. It may affect one ear, both ears, or affect them differently. For some, it develops slowly; for others, it can appear suddenly. A detailed hearing evaluation will show the pattern of your loss and help us create the right treatment plan for you.

Hearing Aid Services

From helping you choose the right hearing aid to providing expert fittings, cleanings, and repairs, we’re here for every step of your hearing journey.

We are committed to ensuring you always enjoy clear, comfortable sound any time, any where.

How do I know which hearing aid is right for me?

The best hearing aid depends on your degree of hearing loss, your daily activities, and your personal preferences. During your consultation, our hearing instrument specialist will explain your options, walk you through the features, and recommend the device that fits both your hearing and your budget.

Do hearing aids need to be professionally fitted?

Yes. A proper fitting makes sure your hearing aids are comfortable and programmed to your hearing needs. Our specialist will adjust your devices based on your test results so they deliver clear, natural sound in everyday situations.

How long does it take to get used to hearing aids?

Everyone adjusts at a different pace. Some people feel comfortable in a few days, while others may need a few weeks. At first, sounds may seem louder or different, but your brain will adapt over time. We’ll support you during the adjustment period and make any needed fine-tuning along the way.

Hearing Aid Fittings

Getting the right fit is the key to hearing aids that feel good and sound their best. Our specialist makes sure your devices are adjusted for your unique hearing loss and lifestyle.

What happens during a hearing aid fitting?

During your fitting, our hearing instrument specialist will program your hearing aids based on your test results. We’ll check that they sit comfortably, adjust them for different listening situations, and teach you how to use and care for them. You’ll also have time to ask questions and practice with your devices.

Why is a proper hearing aid fitting important?

A correct fit ensures your hearing aids provide clear sound without feedback or discomfort. Since every person’s ears and hearing are different, a professional fitting helps you get the most out of your devices and makes adjusting to them much easier.

Will I need follow-up appointments after the fitting?

Yes. Follow-up visits are an important step. These allow us to fine-tune your settings, check how your devices are performing, and answer any questions. Regular follow-ups help make sure you stay comfortable and satisfied with your hearing aids long term.

Hearing Aid Cleaning and Repair

If your hearing aids aren’t working like they should, we’re here to help. We offer fast, dependable cleaning and repair services to keep your devices running smoothly.

How often should I clean my hearing aids?

We recommend wiping them with a soft, dry cloth every day and using the cleaning tools suggested by your provider. Regular care helps prevent wax buildup and extends the life of your devices. During your fitting, we’ll also show you how to clean them properly at home.

What are the signs my hearing aids need repair?

If your devices sound weak, cut in and out, whistle often, or stop working, they may need service. Sometimes a simple cleaning fixes the problem, but if not, bring them to us for a professional check.

Do you offer in-office hearing aid repairs?

Yes. We can handle many common issues in-office, such as tubing changes, filter replacements, or fixing battery doors. If your hearing aid needs to go to the manufacturer, we’ll take care of the process and keep you informed.

Custom Ear Protection

From musicians and swimmers to workers in noisy environments, custom-fit earplugs provide effective, comfortable protection. Our hearing instrument specialist will ensure a perfect fit for your needs.

Who should use custom ear protection?

Anyone exposed to loud sounds or who needs specialized protection can benefit. Musicians, hunters, construction workers, factory employees, swimmers, and even people who want a quieter night’s sleep can find value in custom earplugs.

How is custom ear protection made?

We take an impression of your ear to create a mold designed just for you. This guarantees a snug, comfortable fit and better protection compared to store-bought plugs. Your final product is crafted for your exact needs.

What are the benefits of custom earplugs over store-bought ones?

Custom earplugs fit your ear shape perfectly, making them more comfortable and effective. They block harmful noise while letting safe sounds through, last longer than standard plugs, and can be designed for specific uses like music, swimming, or work.

Hearing Aids & Products

Florida Medical Hearing provides quality hearing aids and products in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we believe better hearing means a better life. That’s why we’re proud to offer and service many of today’s most trusted hearing aid brands. Whether you already use hearing aids or are just beginning to explore your options, our hearing instrument specialist and patient care coordinators are here to help. We take the time to understand your needs, your lifestyle, and your comfort so we can match you with the solution that’s best for you.

Hearing Aid Brands We Trust

Oticon

Oticon has been helping people hear for more than 100 years. Their BrainHearing™ technology works with your brain to make listening feel easier and more natural. Popular models like Oticon Intent and Oticon Real include Bluetooth, tinnitus relief, and clear, natural sound.

Widex

Widex is known for pure, natural sound. Popular models like Widex Moment and Widex SmartRIC connect to smartphones and include Zen Therapy to help with tinnitus. Their newest model, Widex Allure, uses cloud-based fitting technology for an even more customized experience.

Phonak

Phonak is a worldwide leader in hearing care. Models like Phonak Lumity improve speech understanding, while Naída provides strong power for severe hearing loss. The Audéo Sphere Infinio uses advanced AI to cut through background noise, and their new Virto Titanium Infinio offers a custom, discreet fit.

Starkey

Starkey blends great sound with wellness features like fall detection, fitness tracking, and tinnitus support. Models such as Genesis AI and Evolv AI allow hands-free calling, app control, and rechargeability. Their Edge AI line uses smart sound processing to make conversations clearer in real time.

ReSound

ReSound hearing aids are built for natural sound and easy connectivity. Models like ReSound ONE and ReSound Vivia offer rechargeable batteries, smartphone streaming, and tinnitus management. The ReSound Vivia uses AI to improve hearing in noisy settings, while the Enzo IA is a strong, rechargeable option for severe hearing loss.

Unitron

Unitron focuses on comfort and ease of use. Their Blu line automatically adapts to your surroundings, while the new Smile platform uses AI-powered sound tuning and stronger Bluetooth for smoother listening.

Signia

Signia is known for sleek style and powerful technology. Models like Pure Charge&Go and Silk X are small and discreet but deliver clear sound. Features like Own Voice Processing (OVP) make your own voice sound more natural. The Integrated Xperience (IX) platform includes models like Styletto AX and Motion Charge&Go X for advanced performance.

Custom Hearing Protection

We also provide custom-made earplugs for work, hobbies, and daily life. Whether you’re a musician, swimmer, or need protection from loud tools, custom plugs protect your hearing without blocking all sounds.

Custom Ear Molds

Custom ear molds are shaped just for your ears, giving you the best fit and sound quality. They work for hearing aids, in-ear monitors for musicians, headsets, and swim or sleep plugs. A good fit means more comfort, less feedback, and confidence your device will stay in place all day.

Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)

If you struggle to hear on the phone, in noisy places, or while watching TV, ALDs can help. Options like CapTel and CaptionCall phones display real-time captions, making conversations much easier to follow. We also offer TV streamers, personal amplifiers, and Bluetooth microphones to help in specific situations.

FAQs

What is the average cost of hearing aids?

The average cost of hearing aids is between $1,000 and $4,000 per ear, depending on the brand, features, and technology level. Advanced models with Bluetooth streaming, rechargeable batteries, and noise reduction typically cost more than basic models.

What is the difference between hearing aids and over-the-counter devices?

The difference between hearing aids and over-the-counter (OTC) devices is that hearing aids are medical devices professionally fitted to your hearing needs, while OTC devices are general amplifiers for adults with mild to moderate hearing loss. Hearing aids provide custom programming, better sound quality, and long-term support.

How to know which hearing aid is right for me?

The best way to know which hearing aid is right for you is to schedule a professional hearing test with a hearing instrument specialist. Your results will show the type and degree of hearing loss, and the specialist will recommend models that fit your lifestyle, comfort, and budget.

How to take care of hearing aids at home?

The best way to take care of hearing aids at home is to wipe them daily with a dry cloth, store them in a protective case, and keep them away from moisture, heat, and dust. Using a dehumidifier or drying kit at night can also extend their life and performance.

What is the average lifespan of hearing aids?

The average lifespan of hearing aids is about 5 to 7 years with proper care and maintenance. Regular cleaning, battery changes, and professional check-ups can help extend their use, while upgrading sooner may give you access to the latest technology.

Hearing Aids in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL

Let Florida Medical Hearing help you hear clearly, comfortably, and confidently! From trusted hearing aids to custom ear protection and assistive devices, we’ll help you choose the right solution for your life.We offer a wide selection of quality hearing aids in Florida.

Call us today to schedule your appointment with our hearing instrument specialist in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL!

Home » Custom Hearing Protection

Custom Hearing Protection

Florida Medical Hearing provides custom hearing protection in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we believe protecting your hearing is just as important as treating hearing loss. Once your hearing is damaged, it does not come back on its own. That’s why protecting your ears now is one of the smartest choices you can make for your long-term health. Everyday life can be noisy. You may find yourself working with loud machines, going to concerts, enjoying a hunting trip, swimming at the pool, or just trying to get a quiet night’s sleep.

The right hearing protection can make a big difference by keeping your ears safe and reducing your risk of hearing loss. Our friendly hearing instrument specialist will listen to your needs and help you choose hearing protection that feels comfortable, works well, and fits your lifestyle. From simple plugs for sleeping to advanced digital options for hunters and workers, we’ll help you find the solution that’s right for you.

Types of Hearing Protection We Offer

Ready-Made Hearing Protection

These are pre-shaped, affordable options that are easy to find and use for many everyday situations:

  • Earplugs – Small plugs that fit inside the ear canal to reduce noise at concerts, sporting events, or busy public places.
  • Semi-Insert Earplugs – Plugs connected by a band that rest at the entrance of the ear canal. They’re simple to take out and put back in.
  • Earmuffs – Large covers that fit over the ears to block outside sounds.

Note: While ready-made products are convenient, they may not be as comfortable or secure as custom-made protection.

Custom Hearing Protection

Custom protection is made from molds of your ears, giving you a perfect fit, long-lasting comfort, and stronger protection.

  • Custom Earplugs – Perfect for musicians, factory workers, and anyone often exposed to loud sounds.
  • Impulse Filter Earplugs – Great for hunting and shooting. They block sudden loud noises like gunshots but let softer sounds, such as speech, come through clearly.
  • Sleep Plugs – Soft plugs that help reduce snoring and nighttime noise so you can get a restful night’s sleep.
  • In-Ear Monitors (IEMs) – Used by musicians and performers for clear sound without harmful volume levels.
  • Digital Hearing Protection (SoundGear) – Advanced devices for hunters, shooters, and industrial workers that respond instantly to dangerous noise.

Types of Custom Ear Mold Materials

Custom ear molds can be made from different materials depending on your comfort and needs:

  • Acrylic – Hard, smooth, durable, and easy to clean.
  • Vinyl – Softer and gentle on the ear.
  • Silicone – Flexible, water-resistant, and very comfortable for long wear.

FAQs About Custom Hearing Protection

What is the difference between custom earplugs and store-bought earplugs?

The difference between custom earplugs and store-bought earplugs is that custom plugs are molded to the exact shape of your ear, giving you a secure and comfortable fit. Store-bought options are one-size-fits-all and may not block noise as effectively or feel as comfortable for long-term use.

What is the best hearing protection for hunting and shooting?

The best hearing protection for hunting and shooting is impulse filter earplugs or digital shooting protection. These devices block sudden loud gunshot sounds while still allowing you to hear softer noises like voices or movement around you.

How long do custom earplugs last?

Custom earplugs usually last between 3 to 5 years. Their lifespan depends on how often you use them, how well you care for them, and whether the shape of your ears changes over time.

How to clean custom earplugs or ear molds?

The best way to clean custom earplugs or ear molds is to wipe them daily with a soft, dry cloth. For a deeper clean, you can use mild soap and warm water, but make sure they are completely dry before wearing them again. Avoid harsh chemicals, as they can damage the material.

What is the process for getting custom hearing protection?

The process for getting custom hearing protection starts with a quick appointment where a hearing instrument specialist takes an impression of your ear. That mold is sent to a lab, and your custom earplugs or devices are made to fit your ear shape perfectly. The result is protection that is comfortable, secure, and built to last.

Hearing Protection in Sebring, FL & Lake Placid, FL

Florida Medical Hearing offers both ready-made and custom solutions designed just for your needs and comfort.

Call us today to schedule your appointment and explore your options for hearing protection in Lake Placid, FL and Sebring, FL.

Hearing clearly impacts your quality of life now and for years to come in so many ways. Call us today and take the first step toward clearer, more confident listening.

About

Florida Medical Hearing is an audiology and hearing aid clinic with an expert hearing instrument specialist providing hearing tests, sensorineural hearing loss evaluation and treatment, ear wax removal, and professional hearing services in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we know how important hearing is to living fully and staying connected with the people you love. If you’ve been struggling in noisy places, turning up the TV, or missing the little sounds that make life meaningful, we’re here to help. 

We provide complete hearing care, from thorough testing to customized hearing aid fittings, so you can enjoy clearer, more natural hearing every day.

You can trust us to deliver personalized care that puts you first. From our experienced hearing instrument specialist to our caring patient coordinators, we’ll guide you through every step of your journey and make sure you get the support you need for better hearing and a better life.

Our Office Locations

Sebring Lake Placid Medical Hearing

Hearing loss can sneak up on you, making everyday moments harder to enjoy. You might find yourself struggling to follow conversations, shying away from group conversations, feeling tired from the effort of listening, or missing out on the sounds that bring joy to your life. Let our experienced hearing instrument specialist in Sebring and Lake Placid, FL help you rediscover clearer hearing and reconnect with the people and moments that matter most.

Home » Hearing » Rechargeable Hearing Aids: Easy Hearing for Everyday Life

Rechargeable Hearing Aids: Easy Hearing for Everyday Life

  • Debra Adair, HAS
  • February 8, 2026
  • Rechargeable hearing aids give you all-day power, better sound quality, and simple charging without tiny batteries.
  • Many models now include Bluetooth hearing aids technology, phone apps, and smart features that make hearing easier in noisy environments.
  • Florida Medical Hearing offers a wide selection of rechargeable hearing aids in Lake Placid and Sebring, FL.

If you are tired of changing tiny batteries or having your hearing aids die in the middle of the day, it may be time to look at rechargeable hearing aids. These newer products use built-in batteries and simple chargers instead of disposable batteries. Many of them are also Bluetooth hearing aids, which means they can connect to your phone, TV, or tablet for better sound quality and easy volume control.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we help people every day who want hearing that feels easier and more dependable. In this blog, we will explain how rechargeable hearing aids work, what features they offer, how much they may cost, and how to know if they are right for you.

What Are Rechargeable Hearing Aids?

Rechargeable hearing aids are small hearing devices with built-in batteries. Instead of opening a battery door, you put the device into a charging station or charging case. The charger then charges the battery, much like a phone charger.

You can find rechargeable hearing aids in many design styles, including:

  • BTE and RIC styles (behind or on the ear)
  • ITE (in-the-ear) hearing aids that fill part of the ear
  • CIC (completely-in-canal) hearing aids, which are tiny and sit deep in the ear

This variety of size and style makes it easier to match the look and feel you want. Some people like very small CIC devices, while others prefer a slightly larger ITE style that is easier to handle and still offers strong amplification and good clarity.

Many modern rechargeable models are also Bluetooth hearing aids, giving you wireless connectivity to your smartphone and other devices so you can stream calls, TV, music, and podcasts straight to your ears.

How Do Rechargeable Hearing Aids Work?

Inside each rechargeable hearing aid is a special battery made by the manufacturer to last for many charge cycles. When you put your hearing aids into the charger:

  • The charger’s contacts touch the hearing aids.
  • Power flows from the charging station or case into the battery.
  • The battery fills up and stores energy for your next day of usage.

Most people place their pair of hearing aids in the charger at night. By morning, they are ready for a full average day of wearing, even in busy environments with a lot of noise.

Some chargers are portable and durable. They can hold extra power, so you can charge your hearing aids on the go, such as on a trip, even when you do not have an outlet nearby.

Key Features of Modern Rechargeable & Bluetooth Hearing Aids

Today’s rechargeable hearing aids offer many useful features beyond simple power:

1. Bluetooth and App Control

Many models are Bluetooth hearing aids. This means they can connect to your smartphone through an app. With the app, you can:

  • Change volume
  • Switch programs for different environments (quiet room, restaurant, outdoors)
  • Adjust microphone focus to help in background noise
  • Check battery level and rechargeability status

This wireless connectivity can make daily usage much easier and more comfortable.

2. Improved Sound Quality and Clarity

Modern hearing aids include advanced microphones and digital sound processing. They are designed to improve sound quality, clarity, and comfort. Many have feedback reduction to cut down on whistling, and noise reduction to help in busy places like restaurants or stores.

Some models also have tools to help with tinnitus (ringing in the ears), such as sound therapy programs you can adjust using an app.

3. Adjustable and Personalized

Most rechargeable models are highly adjustable. At Florida Medical Hearing, we program each device based on your hearing test results, your age, your lifestyle, and your comfort. We can change settings to match your favorite environments, like church, family dinners, or watching TV.

Rechargeable vs. Battery and OTC Products

You might wonder how rechargeable hearing aids compare with hearing aids that use regular batteries or with OTC (over-the-counter) hearing aid products.

Traditional Battery Hearing Aids

These use small disposable batteries.

  • Pros: You can swap in a fresh battery anywhere; no charger needed.
  • Cons: Ongoing costs of batteries; hard to handle small batteries; less friendly for people with vision or hand problems.

OTC Hearing Aids

OTC devices are sold online or in stores without a hearing test.

  • Pros: Lower price up front, easy to buy.
  • Cons: Limited fitting; may not match your hearing loss; may lack strong support and follow-up services; often weaker sound quality and fewer advanced features like good feedback reduction or custom microphones.

Rechargeable Hearing Aids from a Hearing Clinic

When you get rechargeable hearing aids from a clinic like Florida Medical Hearing:

  • You get a full hearing test before you choose a device.
  • You receive help choosing the right design, size, and technology level.
  • You get follow-up support and services, including cleaning, adjustments, and repairs.

While the price may be higher than some OTC options, the long-term value and quality of care are often much better. Many people who read online reviews later find they still want face-to-face help from a local provider.

Florida Medical Hearing offers a wide selection of hearing aids in Lake Placid, FL and Sebring, FL.

Pros and Cons of Rechargeable Hearing Aids

Like all products, rechargeable hearing aids have pros and cons.

Pros

  • No more buying and changing tiny batteries
  • Good for people with weak hands or poor eyesight
  • Easy daily routine: charge overnight, wear all day
  • Often come as advanced Bluetooth hearing aids with strong connectivity
  • Better for the environment than constantly tossing batteries

Cons

  • You need to remember to charge them
  • If you forget, the aids may not last through the next day
  • You must take care of the charging station or case

For most people, the pros clearly outweigh the cons, especially when you want simple, reliable usage and strong sound quality.

Style, Size, and Design Options

Rechargeable hearing aids come in many shapes and sizes:

  • ITE (in-the-ear) devices that are easy to handle
  • CIC devices that are tiny and very discreet
  • Behind-the-ear models with slim tubes or small microphones behind the ear

The design you choose depends on your hearing loss, finger skills, and what you like to see in the mirror. Some people want the smallest size possible; others prefer a slightly bigger device that is easier to pick up, put in, and adjust.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we will show you a variety of products so you can see and feel them before you decide.

Everyday Usage in Real Environments

Your hearing aids should help you in real life, not just in the office. Rechargeable hearing aids are built to support you in many environments:

  • Quiet rooms at home
  • Restaurants and stores with lots of noise
  • Church or group events
  • Outdoors, where wind might hit the microphone

We adjust your hearing aids so the microphones know how to handle these different places. Many devices automatically change programs as you move between quiet and noisy spots, improving clarity and comfort without you having to press a button.

Costs, Price, and Long-Term Value

The price of rechargeable hearing aids can vary based on:

  • Technology level and features (for example, advanced noise reduction or strong Bluetooth connectivity)
  • Style (CIC, ITE, behind-the-ear, etc.)
  • Brand and manufacturer
  • Warranty, services, and follow-up care included

While the up-front costs can be higher than basic or OTC options, remember that you are not buying disposable batteries all the time, and you are getting professional support and services for the life of the device.

At Florida Medical Hearing, we take time to explain price and payment options clearly. We want you to feel informed and comfortable with your choice.

Florida Medical Hearing: Local Hearing Professionals You Can Trust

Online reviews and ads can only tell you so much. When you come to Florida Medical Hearing in Lake Placid or Sebring, you get real people and real help. We:

  • Test your hearing with care and patience
  • Explain your results and walk through the content in simple words
  • Talk honestly about products, price, and what you can expect
  • Fit and fine-tune your pair of hearing aids for your daily usage
  • Help if you have problems with tinnitus, feedback, or comfort

We are here not just to sell a device, but to be your long-term hearing support team.

Are Rechargeable Hearing Aids Right for You?

You may be a good match for rechargeable hearing aids if you:

  • Want easy, daily power without handling tiny batteries
  • Like the idea of Bluetooth hearing aids with strong connectivity
  • Have hand or vision issues that make small parts hard to use
  • Want better sound quality, clarity, and feedback reduction
  • Prefer a simple routine. Plug into the charger at night, ready in the morning

Even if you are unsure, talking with a hearing care provider can help. We can compare different products, styles, and price levels and help you find the best balance of quality, comfort, and cost.

Rechargeable Hearing Aids in Sebring and Lake Placid, FL

In a world full of noise, it is important to hear the voices and sounds that matter most. Modern rechargeable hearing aids, especially those with Bluetooth connectivity, smart microphones, and strong sound quality, can make that easier than ever.

They offer simple charging, flexible volume control, advanced noise and feedback reduction, and designs that fit many ears and lifestyles.

If you live near Lake Placid or Sebring, FL, and want to learn more about how rechargeable hearing aids could help you or your loved ones, Florida Medical Hearing is ready to help.

Call us today to schedule a hearing test, see different styles in person, and find the device that gives you the comfort, clarity, and confidence you deserve.

Are you ready to hear and be heard?

Hearing clearly impacts your quality of life now and for years to come in so many ways. Call us today and take the first step toward clearer, more confident listening.

Home » Hyperacusis » Hyperacusis: When Everyday Sounds Feel Too Loud

Hyperacusis: When Everyday Sounds Feel Too Loud

  • Debra Adair, HAS
  • January 12, 2026
  • Hyperacusis is a hearing disorder that causes normal sounds to feel painfully loud or overwhelming, even when those sounds are safe for others.
  • Management focuses on improving sound perception and nervous system adaptation, helping the brain react more calmly to everyday noise.
  • Florida Medical Hearing offers personalized hyperacusis consultations and care, guiding patients toward safer listening and better daily comfort.

What Is Hyperacusis?

Hyperacusis is a complex hearing disorder that fundamentally alters how the brain and auditory system react to sound. While most people can filter out the clatter of a restaurant or the hum of an air conditioner, individuals with hyperacusis experience these everyday noises as much louder, sharper, or more uncomfortable than they should be. This problem means sounds like dishes clanking, traffic, or even the strike of a bell can feel physically painful.

It is critical to distinguish that hyperacusis is not simply a matter of “disliking” noise; it is a clinical condition involving sound perception. In many cases, the physical parts of the ear (the eardrum and cochlea) may hear sound normally during a hearing test. However, the brain’s processing reactions are dysfunctional, responding to neutral stimuli as if they were a dangerous threat. 

At Florida Medical Hearing, our audiologists and specialists help patients understand that this experience is a physiological result of auditory dysfunction, not a lack of willpower.

Why Hyperacusis Happens: Causes and Medical History

The development of hyperacusis typically signals that the auditory system has become “hyper-gain” or overly sensitive. Researchers have proposed various theories for this, but it often stems from a combination of factors where the brain’s internal volume control is stuck in the “up” position.

Physical Trauma and Structural Issues

A significant history of physical trauma is a leading cause. This includes:

  • Head Injury: A concussion or severe head trauma can disrupt the neural pathways that regulate sound.
  • Surgery: Complications or the healing process following a middle ear surgery can occasionally trigger sensitivity.
  • ENT System Issues: Structural problems involving the nose, throat, or the ENT system can contribute to pressure imbalances that affect sound perception.

Chronic Diseases and Syndromes

Hyperacusis is frequently a symptom of an underlying medical condition or disease. During a clinical review, doctors look for:

  • Ménière’s Disease: An inner ear disorder that causes vertigo, tinnitus, and fluctuating noise sensitivity.
  • Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) Dysfunction: Because the temporomandibular joint is located so close to the ear canal, jaw misalignment can lead to ear pain and discomfort.
  • Facial Palsy: Conditions like Bell’s palsy can weaken the stapedius muscle, which normally protects the ear from loud noises.
  • Williams Syndrome: This genetic condition often presents with high levels of sound sensitivity from birth.
  • Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Patients with chronic fatigue syndrome often report a generalized sensitivity to sensory input, including sound.

Loudness Exposure

Damage can also occur from acoustic trauma. This might be a single high-decibel exposure, such as fireworks or a gunshot, or long-term exposure to loud music or industrial environments.

The Psychological Impact and Behavioral Reactions

The impact of hyperacusis extends far beyond physical pain; it reshapes a person’s relationship with their environment. When the world feels “too loud,” patients often default to avoidance.

The Cycle of Isolation

Because social situations like parties or grocery shopping become unbearable, individuals may begin to withdraw. This isolation can lead to significant stress, anxiety, and even depression. The emotional effects are compounded when friends and family do not understand why a normal conversation is causing the patient distress.

Fear and Hyperactivity

Over time, the fear of sound can develop into phonophobia (the fear of noise) or misophonia (an intense dislike of specific sounds). This keeps the nervous system in a state of hyperactivity, or a constant “fight-or-flight” mode. In some cases, hyperacusis is a component of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), where specific noises act as triggers for a traumatic history.

Diagnosis and Clinical Evaluation

Seeking a professional evaluation is the first step toward relief. Below is a general process of diagnosing hyperacusis:

  1. Comprehensive Medical History: A detailed review of your past health, including any history of trauma, medications, or previous ear problems.
  2. Advanced Hearing Test: A standard hearing test ensures that your basic hearing rights are intact and determines if hearing loss is a factor.
  3. Loudness Discomfort Level (LDL) Testing: This is the “gold standard” for hyperacusis diagnosis. We carefully measure the exact decibel level where sound shifts from audible to uncomfortable.
  4. Recruitment Evaluation: We check for recruitment, a condition often paired with hearing loss where the ear’s perception of loudness grows much faster than normal.

Hyperacusis Management and Treatment Options

While there is no “magic pill,” the result of modern research shows that the brain is capable of adaptation. Our goal is to retrain your auditory system to handle normal environments again.

Tinnitus Retraining Therapy (TRT)

Tinnitus retraining therapy is a highly effective therapy for hyperacusis. It involves the use of specialized sound-generating devices that provide a low level of background noise. Over time, this constant exposure helps the brain “reset” its tolerance levels.

Counseling and CBT

Education is a powerful tool for recovery. Counseling, specifically Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), helps patients change their emotional reactions to sound. When you understand that a sound is not actually causing physical damage, the brain’s hyperactivity begins to decrease.

Sound Therapy and Lifestyle Advice

  • Sound Generators: Using a wearable device or a tabletop sound machine to avoid total silence.
  • Medications: While not a primary treatment, some medications may be discussed under medical advice to manage the anxiety or stress associated with the disorder.
  • Sleep and Health: Improving general health, such as better sleep hygiene, helps the nervous system stay regulated.

Using Ear Protection Wisely

A common result of hyperacusis is the over-use of earplugs. While it feels like the right thing to do, wearing protection in quiet environments actually makes the brain more sensitive. This “over-protection” causes the brain to further increase its internal volume to hear, worsening the condition.

Doctors and audiologists recommend using ear protection only in truly dangerous environments. Your specialist will provide specific information on a “weaning” schedule to help you rebuild your tolerance safely.

Contact Florida Medical Hearing to be connected with a hearing aid specialist in Lake Placid, FL or Sebring, FL.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is hyperacusis related to tinnitus? 

Yes. They are often viewed as two sides of the same coin. Both involve a perception problem in the brain. Tinnitus retraining therapy and sound therapy are the primary treatments used for both.

Can children or adolescents develop this? 

Yes. Adolescents can develop hyperacusis due to loud music exposure or underlying syndromes like Williams syndrome. Early education and care are vital for younger patients.

What is the role of an otolaryngologist? 

An otolaryngologist (ENT doctor) performs the medical evaluation to ensure there are no physical diseases or problems in the ear canal or middle ear that require surgery or specific medicine.

How long does adaptation take? 

Adaptation is a “slow and steady” process. Most individuals see a noticeable result within several months of consistent therapy and sound therapy.

Moving Forward with Florida Medical Hearing

Hyperacusis can make the world feel like a hostile place, but you don’t have to face it alone. With the right combination of clinical evaluation, tinnitus retraining therapy, and expert support, you can regain your quality of life.

If you are ready to take the next step toward relief, contact Florida Medical Hearing today for a comprehensive consultation.

Our team is dedicated to providing the care, information, and advice you need to navigate this journey with confidence.

Are you ready to hear and be heard?

Hearing clearly impacts your quality of life now and for years to come in so many ways. Call us today and take the first step toward clearer, more confident listening.

Hearing Aids for Tinnitus: A Simple Guide to Relief and Better Hearing

  • Debra Adair, HAS
  • December 18, 2025

Tinnitus is the ringing, buzzing, or hissing sound you hear when no one else can. This article explains how hearing aids for tinnitus can help by improving hearing and making tinnitus less noticeable. You’ll also learn what to expect during an evaluation and how Florida Medical Hearing Aids in Sebring and Lake Placid, FL can help you find the right plan.

If you deal with ringing in your ears, you may wonder if anything truly helps. The good news is that hearing aids for tinnitus can make a big difference for many people. Tinnitus often feels louder when the world around you sounds quiet. When hearing aids boost real sounds, like voices, water running, or birds outside, your brain has more to focus on, and tinnitus can feel less strong. 

At Florida Medical Hearing Aids in Sebring and Lake Placid, FL, we help patients understand what’s causing their tinnitus and whether hearing aids could bring relief.

This guide keeps things simple. We’ll explain what tinnitus is, why it happens, how hearing aids can help, and what steps you can take next.

What Is Tinnitus?

Tinnitus is the sound you hear inside your ears or head when there is no outside sound making it. People describe tinnitus in different ways, such as:

  • Ringing
  • Buzzing
  • Hissing
  • Whistling
  • Clicking
  • Roaring

Some people hear it all the time. Others notice it more at night or in quiet rooms. Tinnitus is not a disease by itself. Most of the time, it is a symptom that something is affecting your hearing system.

Why Does Tinnitus Happen?

Tinnitus has many possible causes. Some are simple and temporary. Others need professional care. Common causes include:

Hearing Loss

This is one of the biggest reasons people have tinnitus. When the ear has trouble picking up sound, the brain may “turn up the volume” and tinnitus can become more noticeable.

Loud Noise Exposure

Loud music, power tools, firearms, or long-term workplace noise can damage tiny hair cells in the inner ear.

Earwax Buildup

Wax can block sound from reaching the eardrum. This can make hearing muffled and may trigger tinnitus for some people.

Ear Infections or Fluid

A middle ear infection or fluid behind the eardrum can change how you hear and may cause ringing.

Stress, Anxiety, and Poor Sleep

When your body is stressed, tinnitus can feel louder. Lack of sleep can also make it harder to cope with tinnitus.

Certain Medications or Health Conditions

Some medications may affect hearing, and certain health issues can increase tinnitus symptoms. A proper evaluation helps sort this out.

Do Hearing Aids Help Tinnitus?

For many people, yes, especially if tinnitus is linked to hearing loss. Here’s why: tinnitus often becomes stronger when you are not hearing enough real sound. Quiet rooms make tinnitus stand out, like a bright light in a dark space.

Hearing aids for tinnitus help by increasing the sound around you. This can reduce how “big” tinnitus feels in your day.

How Hearing Aids for Tinnitus Work

Hearing aids don’t “cure” tinnitus, but they can help you manage it in a real, practical way. Here are the main benefits:

1) They Make Speech and Everyday Sound Clearer

When you can hear voices better, your brain doesn’t work as hard. That can reduce listening fatigue, frustration, and stress, which can also help tinnitus feel less intense.

2) They Reduce the “Silence” That Makes Tinnitus Stand Out

Many patients say tinnitus is worst when everything is quiet. Hearing aids bring in normal sound so tinnitus blends into the background more easily.

3) Many Hearing Aids Include Masking Features

Some hearing aids include tinnitus programs that play soft relief sounds, such as:

  • White noise
  • Gentle ocean sounds
  • Soft static-like sounds
  • Calm tones

This is called masking. The goal is not to drown out tinnitus, but to make it less noticeable and less annoying.

4) They Help Your Brain “Re-Train” What It Pays Attention To

Over time, the brain can learn to focus less on tinnitus and more on real sound, especially when hearing is improved and sound therapy is used.

Who Is a Good Candidate for Hearing Aids for Tinnitus?

You may be a good fit if:

  • You have tinnitus and also have hearing loss
  • You struggle to hear speech clearly, especially in noise
  • Your tinnitus is worse in quiet places
  • You feel stressed or tired from the constant sound
  • You want a long-term plan that supports both hearing and comfort

Even if you are not sure you have hearing loss, a hearing evaluation can confirm it.

Florida Medical Hearing provides hearing tests in Sebring, FL and Lake Placid, FL.

What If I Don’t Have Hearing Loss?

Some people have tinnitus without clear hearing loss on a basic screening. In those cases, hearing aids may still help if:

  • You have hidden hearing difficulties (like trouble hearing in noise)
  • You need masking sounds to reduce tinnitus awareness
  • You benefit from a sound-therapy plan

An in-depth evaluation will help decide what is most helpful for you.

What to Expect at a Tinnitus and Hearing Evaluation

At Florida Medical Hearing Aids, we focus on making sure you feel heard and supported. A tinnitus visit often includes:

Step 1: Getting to Know You

We listen to your story. We ask about:

  • When tinnitus started
  • What it sounds like
  • When it feels worse
  • Noise exposure history
  • Sleep and stress
  • Your hearing goals

Step 2: Ear Exam

We check for:

  • Earwax buildup
  • Signs of infection or irritation
  • Blockage that may affect hearing

Step 3: Hearing Testing

You may complete tests that measure:

  • How soft a sound you can hear
  • How well you understand speech
  • How your ears and middle ear are working

Step 4: Clear Explanation of Results

We review results in simple terms. If hearing loss is present, we explain what it means for everyday life.

Step 5: Personalized Plan

If hearing aids are a good option, we discuss what features matter most, especially tinnitus masking, comfort, and ease of use.

What Features Should I Look for in Hearing Aids for Tinnitus?

Not every hearing aid is the same. Helpful features for tinnitus often include:

Tinnitus Sound Support 

These programs play steady, soothing sounds to help reduce tinnitus awareness.

Strong Noise Management

This helps you hear speech better in restaurants, family gatherings, and busy places.

Directional Microphones

They help focus on the person talking in front of you.

Rechargeable Options

Rechargeable hearing aids are simple and convenient—no tiny batteries to change.

App Controls

Many hearing aids let you adjust volume and tinnitus programs from a smartphone app.

Comfortable Fit

Comfort matters. If a hearing aid feels uncomfortable, you won’t want to wear it, and consistent use is key for tinnitus support.

How Long Does It Take to Get Relief?

Relief can happen in different ways:

  • Some patients notice improvement quickly, especially with masking programs.
  • Others notice gradual improvement over weeks as the brain adapts.
  • The best results often come from consistent daily wear and follow-up adjustments.

Tinnitus management is more like a plan than a one-time fix. We’re here to guide you through that plan.

Other Ways to Support Tinnitus Relief (Along With Hearing Aids)

Hearing aids work even better when you also support your overall well-being. Simple steps include:

Protect Your Ears From Loud Noise

Use ear protection for loud tools, concerts, or mowing.

Use Soft Background Sound at Night

A fan, sound machine, or gentle music can reduce the “quiet” that makes tinnitus worse.

Focus on Sleep

Poor sleep can make tinnitus feel louder. Setting a steady bedtime routine can help.

Manage Stress

Stress doesn’t cause tinnitus for everyone, but it often makes it worse. Breathing exercises, short walks, or calming routines can help.

Watch for Triggers

Some people notice tinnitus changes with caffeine, alcohol, or high-salt foods. Tracking your symptoms can help you learn patterns.

Common Myths About Hearing Aids and Tinnitus

Myth: “Hearing aids will cure tinnitus.”

Truth: They usually don’t cure it, but they can reduce how much it bothers you.

Myth: “If tinnitus is loud, hearing aids won’t help.”

Truth: Many people with loud tinnitus still get relief when hearing improves and masking is used.

Myth: “I should wait until tinnitus gets worse.”

Truth: Early help can prevent frustration, sleep problems, and extra stress.

When Should I Seek Help?

Reach out for an evaluation if:

  • Tinnitus lasts more than a week or two
  • You have hearing trouble or muffled sound
  • Tinnitus affects sleep, focus, or mood
  • You feel anxious or overwhelmed by the noise
  • Ringing is only in one ear
  • You have pain, drainage, or sudden hearing changes

If tinnitus starts suddenly or comes with sudden hearing loss, seek medical care right away.

Hearing Aids for Tinnitus in Sebring and Lake Placid, FL

Living with tinnitus can feel isolating, but you are not alone. With the right treatment plan, many people feel calmer, more confident, and more connected again. Hearing aids can improve communication and reduce the stress that tinnitus creates.

At Florida Medical Hearing Aids in Sebring and Lake Placid, we take time to understand what you’re hearing and how it affects your life. If hearing loss is part of the problem, hearing aids for tinnitus may be one of the best tools to help you move forward. We will guide you through testing, fitting, and follow-up care, and we’ll adjust your settings until they feel comfortable for your daily life.

If ringing in your ears is wearing you down, don’t wait. Call Florida Medical Hearing Aids today to schedule your hearing and tinnitus evaluation in Sebring or Lake Placid, FL and take the first step toward clearer hearing and real relief. 

Hearing aids for tinnitus may help you feel more in control again, one day at a time.

Are you ready to hear and be heard?

Hearing clearly impacts your quality of life now and for years to come in so many ways. Call us today and take the first step toward clearer, more confident listening.
